world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Surreal locations with impossible architecture; blended real/unreal aesthetics; isometric perspective emphasizing disorientation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Max assumes alternate identities (Sarah, Grimwall); NPCs behave according to dreamlogic; narrative causality is non-linear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bandaged protagonist; fragmented environmental design; unclear spatial coherence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Max gradually recovers first name; puzzles require exploration to unlock narrative; dialogue reveals incremental memory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sarah persona overlay; childhood imagery; sickness/disease visual metaphors
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Max experiences identity shift; confronts emotional barriers; emotional puzzle-solving required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dr. Morgan appears as threatening figure; medical imagery weaponized; institutional setting corrupted
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final confrontation requires Max to overcome psychological representation of Morgan; climactic injection scene

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Return to real-world hospital; intravenous line removal; family reunion setting; color/clarity shift from dream aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Max regains agency; successful cure publication; narrative resolution; life progression (birth of child)

environmental_storytelling.example: Each chapter (malformed children town, demented circus, alien hive, Aztec village) presents distinct visual/atmospheric markers that communicate different psychological states and thematic focuses without explicit exposition
Dilapidated sanitarium visuals communicate institutional failure, mental deterioration, and loss of safety—reinforcing Max's unstable mental state
When Max assumes personas (Sarah, Grimwall), environment and character visuals shift to reflect his altered perception—the space itself reflects his mental identity
Isometric perspective, impossible architecture, and surreal entity designs create persistent uncertainty about what is 'real' vs. imagined, communicating Max's cognitive state
The final confrontation uses Dr. Morgan's injection imagery to represent Max's internal conflict—visual language converts healthcare into threat
Aztec village, alien hive, and circus settings each carry distinct visual vocabularies that signal different narrative/thematic zones within Max's fractured consciousness

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Presides over malformed children town; malevolent surveillance posture
Terrorizes demented circus inhabitants; environmental hazard behavior
Populate alien hive; biomechanical coordination; dehumanization threat
Devastates Aztec village; unstoppable destructive force; mythological agency
Explores corridors seeking answers; gradually regains identity; solves puzzles to unlock narrative
Visualizes Dr. Morgan threat; performs agency action (IV line removal); breaks from coma through will
Appears as injector/threat figure; represents Max's guilt and professional conflict; serves as final psychological obstacle

notes: Sanitarium uses psychological horror as its primary manifestation layer. The game's central mechanic—blending real and imagined worlds—means visual communication must consistently signal uncertainty. Danger is communicated not through combat mechanics but through environmental grotesquerie and identity dissolution. The protagonist's bandaged face and amnesia establish visual vulnerability. World-state transitions are marked by cinematic sequences and chapter progression, creating visual anchors in an otherwise disorienting narrative. The game's horror derives from visual manifestation of psychological concepts: malformed children = guilt about Sarah; Dr. Morgan = professional anxiety; alien entities = incomprehensible threats to autonomy. Faction identity is minimal; instead, the game uses entity presence and environmental association to communicate alignment (Mercy Foundation = healing/cure; Morgan = profit/conflict; Mother = alien domination).
